svg.text: A structured approach for knowing when enough is enough

g.text: Phase 1 — Define your ship-quality bar (do once)
For a portfolio twin, the bar is NOT "perfect answers." It's:
A. Accurate — doesn't say things about you that aren't true
B. Complete — covers your key projects, background, and interests
C. Graceful — handles unknowns without hallucinating (Pushover fires!)
D. Engaging — someone asks 3+ questions, doesn't bounce after 1
Phase 2 — Run your fixed test set (you already built this!)
Your audit_retrieval.py has 20 prompts (10 recruiter + 10 friendly).
Run each through admin_app. For each response, score A/B/C/D:
Pass (the answer would make you proud if a recruiter saw it)
Flag (not wrong, but could be better — note what's weak)
Fail (hallucination, missed a key fact, or incoherent)
Phase 3 — Sweep one parameter at a time
Pick the 3-5 FLAGGED questions. Re-run them with each change:
1. top_k: try 5 vs 8 vs 12. Does retrieval diversity improve?
2. model: same question, gpt-4.1 vs claude-sonnet vs gemini-flash.
Does tone/accuracy shift? Is the cheaper model "good enough"?
3. temperature: 0.7 vs 1.0. Lower = more consistent, higher = warmer.
Change ONE thing. If it helps, keep it. If ambiguous, keep the default.
Phase 4 — The ship decision
Ship when: 18+ of 20 test prompts pass. No fails. Flags are cosmetic.
Keep investigating if: any FAIL exists, or the Pushover "I don't know"
fires on a core topic (projects, background, skills).
Defer to "future sprint" if: flag is about tone, not facts.
Record your decisions (the admin metadata tab is your lab notebook)
For each parameter change: what you tried, what changed, whether you kept it.
This becomes a "tuning log" in your portfolio — shows your process, not just results.
The diminishing returns signal
If you're re-running the same question with 3+ model/parameter combos
and the answers all seem "fine but different" — that's the signal. Ship it.
